title: Shipfix-Global Market Update
summary:
Main
By Ulf Bergman

Macro/Geopolitics
Last week ended with signs that the US labour market is slowing down amid mounting economic headwinds and rising interest rates. The world's largest economy added 209,000 jobs in June, the lowest since December 2020. However, the country's unemployment rate retreated marginally despite the weaker-than-expected reading.

children.title: Macro/Geopolitics
children.summary: Last week ended with signs that the US labour market is slowing down amid mounting economic headwinds and rising interest rates. The world's largest economy added 209,000 jobs in June, the lowest since December 2020.

children.title: Commodity Markets
children.summary: Following a robust session on Friday, crude oil prices advanced over the course of the past week as concerns over tight global supplies and a weaker dollar supported the prices. The September Brent futures recorded a weekly advance of 4.1 per cent and ended Friday's session at 78.47 dollars per barrel.

children.title: Freight and Bunker Markets
children.summary: Despite gains for the larger segments on Thursday and Friday, the Baltic Dry Index recorded a sharp decline last week. The headline index shed 7.5 per cent amid weekly losses across all dry bulk segments.

children.title: The View from the Shipfix Desk
section_path: The View from the Shipfix Desk
children.section_path_text: The View from the Shipfix Desk
children.summary: Cargo order volumes for Russian coal loading in the Baltic Sea remained under pressure during the past month. The loss of the European market in the wake of sanctions initially saw an increase in shipments to the distant ports in Asia.
